message.content: Adversaries may exploit Domain Policy Modification to evade defenses and escalate privileges within domain environments. This technique involves altering domain Group Policy Objects (GPOs) or trust settings, including federation trusts, to manipulate how computer resources interact on a network. With sufficient permissions, attackers can modify these settings to execute a variety of attacks, such as pushing malicious Scheduled Tasks across the domain or incorporating an adversary-controlled domain to manipulate access tokens. They may also implement a Rogue Domain Controller by changing Active Directory (AD) configuration settings. The potential impact is significant, as it can lead to widespread compromise of domain resources and persistent access. Common exploitation methods include modifying GPOs, altering domain trusts, and temporarily changing domain policies to carry out malicious actions before reverting changes to avoid detection. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strict access controls, regularly auditing domain policy changes, and monitoring for unusual modifications to GPOs and trust settings. Implementing robust logging and alerting mechanisms can also help detect and respond to suspicious activities promptly.
